---
title: "Carolina Redroot (Lachnanthes caroliana)"
slug: "lachnanthes-caroliana"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622601334/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Carolina Redroot"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Commelinales"
tax_family: "Haemodoraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Commelinales", "Haemodoraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622601334/medium.jpg" alt="Carolina Redroot" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/marykeim">
marykeim</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341946648">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Commelinales
-   **Family:** Haemodoraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=164401)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   **IUCN:** Least Concern
-   **Rarity note:** Common across Florida.

## Natural history notes

A wetland plant characterized by its narrow, grass-like leaves and dense clusters of small, woolly white flowers. In Florida, it is found in acidic, sandy wetlands such as bogs, savannas, and wet flatwoods. It typically blooms from late spring through summer, and its reddish roots give the plant its common name.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/164401-Lachnanthes-caroliana)
-   [IUCN Red List](https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/64316895/67729937)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-lachnanthes-caroliana/)
